section.title: Remember the LORD
section.sectionRationale: Moses turns from the severity of chapter 7 to the discipline of memory, retelling how God humbled and fed Israel in the wilderness to teach dependence on His word, and warning that the coming abundance of the land is a subtler danger than the hardship of the desert ever was.
section.thesis: Moses calls Israel to remember the whole of their wilderness journey, where God humbled and tested them with hunger and manna to teach that life comes from every word of His mouth, and warns that the abundance of the good land ahead poses the greater danger: forgetting the LORD who alone gives the power to gain wealth.
section.frameNote: Four movements: remembering the whole way, humbled and tested; the good land ahead; the warning against forgetting God in prosperity; and the reminder that it is the LORD who gives power to gain wealth.

units.label: Remember the whole way, humbled and tested
units.note: man does not live on bread alone

verses.text: You must carefully follow every commandment I am giving you today, so that you may live and multiply, and enter and possess the land that the LORD swore to give your fathers.

Remember that these forty years the LORD your God led you all the way in the wilderness, so that He might {{anah|humble}} you and test you in order to know what was in your heart, whether or not you would keep His commandments.

He humbled you, and in your hunger He gave you manna to eat, which neither you nor your fathers had known, so that you might understand that man does not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD.
Your clothing did not wear out and your feet did not swell during these forty years.
So know in your heart that just as a man disciplines his son, so the LORD your God disciplines you.

units.label: The good land ahead
units.note: a land where you will lack nothing

verses.text: Therefore you shall keep the commandments of the LORD your God, walking in His ways and fearing Him.
For the LORD your God is bringing you into a good land, a land of brooks and fountains and springs that flow through the valleys and hills;
a land of wheat, barley, vines, fig trees, and pomegranates; a land of olive oil and honey;
a land where you will eat food without scarcity, where you will lack nothing; a land whose rocks are iron and whose hills are ready to be mined for copper.
When you eat and are satisfied, you are to bless the LORD your God for the good land that He has given you.
Be careful not to {{shakach|forget}} the LORD your God by failing to keep His commandments and ordinances and statutes, which I am giving you this day.
Otherwise, when you eat and are satisfied, when you build fine houses in which to dwell,
and when your herds and flocks grow large and your silver and gold increase and all that you have is multiplied,
then your heart will become proud, and you will forget the LORD your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.
He led you through the vast and terrifying wilderness with its venomous snakes and scorpions, a thirsty and waterless land. He brought you water from the rock of flint.
He fed you in the wilderness with manna that your fathers had not known, in order to humble you and test you, so that in the end He might cause you to prosper.
You might say in your heart, “The power and strength of my hands have made this wealth for me.”

units.label: Remember who gives the power to gain wealth

verses.text: But remember that it is the LORD your God who gives you the {{koach|power}} to gain wealth, in order to confirm His covenant that He swore to your fathers even to this day.
If you ever forget the LORD your God and go after other gods to worship and bow down to them, I testify against you today that you will surely perish.
Like the nations that the LORD has destroyed before you, so you will perish if you do not obey the LORD your God.

anah.gloss: To humble, afflict, or bring low. Describes God's purposeful use of hardship, hunger in this case, not as cruelty but as discipline meant to reveal and reshape what is truly in a person's heart.

shakach.gloss: To forget, to cease remembering or acting on what one knows. In this chapter it describes the specific danger of prosperity: not doctrinal denial of God but a practical, functional amnesia that lets comfort crowd out active dependence on Him.

koach.gloss: Power, strength, ability. Used of the very capacity to work and produce wealth, undercutting any claim to self-made success by naming even one's productive strength itself as a gift from God.

basic.context: Moses looks back over forty years of wilderness wandering, explaining that God humbled Israel with hunger and then fed them with manna to teach dependence on His word, and now warns that the abundance of the good land ahead is actually a greater spiritual danger than the hardship of the wilderness ever was.
basic.hermeneutics: This is both memory and warning: a real history of hunger and provision that teaches a timeless truth, prosperity more easily produces forgetfulness of God than hardship does. Its call to actively remember God's provision applies directly to believers today, even though the specific wilderness details belong to Israel's unique history.
basic.originalLanguages: God used anah, humbling or affliction, purposefully, to test what was truly in Israel's heart. The chapter's central danger is that Israel would shakach, forget, God amid prosperity, wrongly crediting their own koach, strength or power, for wealth that was always a gift from Him.
basic.typology: Israel's wilderness testing over manna and bread anticipates and is directly recalled by Jesus' own wilderness temptation, where He is tested with hunger yet refuses to turn stones to bread, quoting this very chapter, Matthew 4:4, succeeding perfectly where Israel had so often failed.
basic.christ: When tempted to turn stones into bread after forty days of fasting, Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 8:3 directly, refusing to let physical need override trust in God's word, succeeding as the true and faithful Israel where the nation itself had so often failed in the wilderness.
basic.god: God disciplines His people as a father disciplines a son, using hardship purposefully rather than randomly, and He is also the generous giver of every good thing in the land ahead, including the very strength and ability by which people produce wealth.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 4:1-4 records Jesus quoting this chapter directly in His own wilderness temptation. Proverbs 30:8-9 echoes the same danger of both poverty and prosperity leading a heart away from God. 1 Timothy 6:17-19 warns the rich not to put their hope in wealth but in God.
basic.oneStory: This chapter names one of the deepest, most ordinary temptations of the faithful life: forgetting the God who provides once His provision becomes abundant and familiar. Christ succeeds perfectly where Israel failed, and His victory over the same temptation gives His people both an example and the grace to remember rightly.

context.literary: This chapter forms a bridge between the severe warnings of chapter 7 and the historical confessions of chapter 9, using the discipline of remembering as its central theme: remember the humbling, remember the provision, remember who gives the power to prosper.
context.historical: Addressed to a generation who had grown up on manna in the wilderness and was about to encounter settled agricultural abundance for the first time, this chapter anticipates precisely the temptation such a transition would bring.
context.canonical: Deuteronomy 8:3 becomes one of the most quoted verses in the New Testament, directly cited by Jesus in His own wilderness temptation, making this chapter's theology of dependence a direct link between Israel's story and Christ's.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To root Israel's future obedience in the practiced discipline of active remembering, teaching that the hardship of the wilderness was purposeful discipline and warning that the coming prosperity of the land carries the greater risk of forgetting God altogether.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific historical details, manna, forty years, this particular land's produce, are descriptive of Israel's unique wilderness history. What is directly prescriptive and abiding is the pattern itself: humbling and testing reveal the heart, and remembering God's provision must be a deliberate, ongoing practice, not an automatic reflex.

debates.question: Was the wilderness hunger described in verses 2-3 primarily a punishment or a discipline?

views.label: Primarily fatherly discipline, not punitive judgment
views.case: Verse 5 explicitly frames it as a father disciplining a son, language of loving formation rather than punishment for a specific offense, aimed at revealing and shaping the heart.

views.label: It includes an element of consequence for the generation's earlier unbelief
views.case: Given chapters 1 and 9's emphasis on the previous generation's rebellion, some read the wilderness hardship as also carrying a corrective, consequence-bearing dimension tied to Israel's earlier unbelief at Kadesh-barnea.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the wilderness experience was purposeful rather than random, and both affirm that its ultimate aim, stated in verse 16, was that God 'might cause you to prosper' in the end.

study.originalLanguages: God's anah, humbling, of Israel through hunger was purposeful testing meant to expose and reshape the heart, and the great danger named in this chapter is that prosperity would cause Israel to shakach, forget, the LORD, crediting their own koach, strength or power, for wealth that was always His gift.

typology.type: Israel tested with hunger in the wilderness
typology.fulfillment: Directly recalled and fulfilled when Jesus, tested with hunger after forty days, quotes this very chapter and succeeds where Israel failed, Matthew 4:1-4.

typology.type: Manna, bread not previously known, sustaining Israel
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ, the true bread from heaven, who gives life to the world, John 6:32-35.

study.christ: Jesus, tested with real hunger after forty days in the wilderness, is offered the same temptation Israel faced, bread for physical need, and quotes Deuteronomy 8:3 directly in refusing it, Matthew 4:4, succeeding perfectly as the true and faithful Israel where the nation had so often failed. He later calls Himself the true bread from heaven, John 6:32-35, fulfilling what manna could only foreshadow.
study.god: God disciplines His people purposefully, as a father disciplines a son, using even hardship to reveal and reshape the heart, and He is also the generous source of every good thing in the land ahead, including the very strength and capacity by which people are able to work and prosper.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 8:3 directly when tempted with hunger in His own wilderness testing.
Jesus calls Himself the true bread from heaven, fulfilling what manna could only foreshadow.
Echoes the same danger that both poverty and prosperity can lead a heart away from God.
Warns the wealthy not to place their hope in riches but in the God who richly provides.

study.oneStory: This chapter names one of the most ordinary and dangerous temptations of the faithful life: forgetting the God who provides once His provision becomes abundant and familiar. Christ succeeds perfectly where Israel failed under the same test, and His victory gives His people both a pattern to imitate and the grace to remember rightly.

sermon.title: Not by Bread Alone
sermon.why: Keller preaches directly through this chapter's warning about prosperity and forgetting God, drawing out how dependence on 'every word from the mouth of the LORD' is meant to shape practical, daily life after salvation, not only survival in the wilderness.

quiz.q: Why did God let Israel hunger and then feed them manna in the wilderness (v.3)?

options.text: To humble them and teach that man does not live on bread alone but on every word from the mouth of the LORD.
options.note: Verse 3 gives this exact reason, later quoted by Jesus in Matthew 4:4.

options.text: To punish them for a specific unnamed sin.
options.note: Verse 2 frames this as humbling and testing, not punishment for a specific named offense.

options.text: Because there was no other food available anywhere in the region.
options.note: The text presents the hunger and manna as purposeful teaching, not merely a logistical necessity.

options.text: No reason is given for the manna or the hunger.
options.note: Verse 3 gives an explicit, stated purpose.

quiz.q: How does Jesus use Deuteronomy 8:3 in the Gospels?

options.text: He quotes it directly when tempted by the devil to turn stones into bread, refusing to prioritize physical need over God's word.
options.note: Matthew 4:4 records Jesus quoting this verse word for word in His wilderness temptation.

options.text: He rejects it as no longer relevant.
options.note: Jesus affirms and applies it directly to His own temptation, the opposite of rejecting it.

options.text: He reinterprets it to mean bread is entirely unnecessary.
options.note: The verse and Jesus' use of it affirm bread's real necessity while placing greater weight on dependence on God's word.

options.text: Jesus never references this verse.
options.note: Matthew 4:4 records a direct quotation.

quiz.q: What specific danger does Moses warn will come with prosperity in the good land (vv.12-14)?

options.text: That when houses, herds, and wealth multiply, Israel's heart will become proud and forget the LORD who brought them out of Egypt.
options.note: Verses 12-14 describe this exact progression from abundance to pride to forgetting God.

options.text: That the land itself would become physically dangerous to live in.
options.note: The danger described is spiritual and internal, pride and forgetting, not a physical threat from the land.

options.text: That prosperity itself is inherently sinful and must be avoided.
options.note: The chapter presents prosperity as a genuine blessing from God, verses 7-10, while warning about its spiritual dangers.

options.text: No dangers of prosperity are mentioned in this chapter.
options.note: Verses 12-14 give a detailed, specific warning.

quiz.q: What does Moses say Israel must remember about the source of their ability to gain wealth (v.18)?

options.text: That it is the LORD who gives the power to gain wealth, in order to confirm His covenant.
options.note: Verse 18 states this directly, countering the claim in verse 17 that human strength alone produced the wealth.

options.text: That wealth comes entirely from human skill and effort alone.
options.note: Verse 17 presents this as the very claim Moses is correcting, not the truth he is affirming.

options.text: That wealth is randomly distributed with no cause at all.
options.note: Verse 18 names a specific source, the LORD, rather than randomness.

options.text: Nothing is said about the source of wealth.
options.note: Verse 18 makes an explicit statement about its source.

quiz.q: What warning does Moses give in verses 19-20 about forgetting the LORD and following other gods?

options.text: Israel would surely perish, just like the nations the LORD destroyed before them.
options.note: Verses 19-20 state this warning explicitly and starkly.

options.text: Israel would simply be fined a portion of their crops.
options.note: The stated consequence is far more severe, complete destruction, not a fine.

options.text: Nothing would happen once Israel was settled and secure in the land.
options.note: The text warns the opposite; settled security is precisely when this danger is greatest.

options.text: No warning at all is given in these verses.
options.note: Verses 19-20 give an explicit and severe warning.

questions: God let Israel go hungry before feeding them manna, to teach dependence on His word rather than bread alone. Where might God be humbling you right now to teach a deeper kind of dependence?

Jesus quoted this very verse when tempted in the wilderness, refusing to let a legitimate physical need override trust in God's word. Where are you tempted to meet a real need in a way that bypasses trust in God?

Moses warns that 'when you have eaten and are satisfied' is exactly when Israel's heart is most likely to grow proud. How has comfort or success in your own life quietly produced more self-reliance than gratitude?
Moses commands Israel to actively remember the LORD's provision, an intentional discipline, not an accident. What specific practices help you remember, rather than forget, what God has done for you?
The chapter ends with a stark warning that forgetting God leads to ruin. What is currently competing for the trust and attention that belongs only to God in your life right now?

translationNotes.note: This verse is quoted word for word by Jesus in Matthew 4:4 during His own wilderness temptation, making it one of the most directly fulfilled verses in Deuteronomy.
'Venomous snakes and scorpions' names real, concrete dangers of the Sinai wilderness, grounding the chapter's lesson about dependence in genuine historical hardship, not abstract metaphor.
'The power to gain wealth' identifies even human strength, skill, and ability itself as a gift from God, undercutting any claim to being entirely self-made.
